1: David arrives at Nob, the priestly city, alone. wayyeḥaraḏ ʾAḥîmeleḵ liqrāʾṯ Dāwîḏ — “Ahimelech trembled to meet David.” ḥāraḏ (H2729, to tremble/be frightened) signals that the priest senses something wrong in David’s solitary arrival — no royal escort, no explanation. The question “why are you alone, and no one with you?” is professional, not merely curious.
2: dāḇār ṣiwwānî hammelek — “the king has commanded me on a matter.” David’s lie protects him at the cost of implicating Ahimelech in an act of treason (aiding an unauthorized person). ûmāqôm muʿad yāḏaʿtî ʾeṯ-hannəʿārîm — “and I have told the young men the appointed place” — David invents a detachment of soldiers waiting elsewhere; the fiction provides cover for the request that follows.
3: The request for bread: ḥămišāh-leḥem tēn bəyāḏî ʾô hammimṣāʾ — “give me five loaves in my hand, or whatever is available.” Five loaves is a substantial request; the “or whatever” signals real hunger and urgency. The number five will reappear in the NT feeding narratives (Matthew 14:17).
4: ʾên-leḥem ḥōl ʾel-taḥaṯ yāḏî kî-ʾim-leḥem qōdeš — “there is no ordinary bread under my hand, but there is holy bread.” The ḥōl vs. qōdeš distinction (profane/ordinary vs. holy/consecrated) is the central tension of the episode. The bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm, Exodus 25:30; Leviticus 24:5-9) was ritually reserved — replaced weekly, eaten by priests only. Ahimelech’s conditional offer — if the men have kept from women — applies Numbers 5 purity logic to the emergency.
5: ʾāḵēn ḥullǎlāh libbênûš miṯmôl šilšôm — “indeed, women have been kept from us since yesterday and the day before.” David’s claim of ritual purity for his men is the halachic justification. Jesus will cite this episode (Matthew 12:3-4; Mark 2:25-26; Luke 6:3-4) as a precedent that human need can override ceremonial law: David’s act is cited as lawful by the Son of David — an implicit claim that he has authority to interpret Torah.
6: wayyittēn-lô hakkōhēn qōdeš — “the priest gave him holy bread.” leḥem happānîm (bread of the Presence/Face) was the weekly bread set out before YHWH, renewed each Sabbath (Leviticus 24:8); removed bread was hot — just replaced. The hot bread given to David is the bread of the divine presence — the immediate, daily sustenance of YHWH, now given to the fugitive king. The irony: the man anointed to rule is fed by the bread of the Lord’s own table.
7: āḏōm ʾeḏōmî — Doeg the Edomite, “detained before YHWH.” ʿāṣûr lipnê YHWH (detained/kept before YHWH) may mean he is undergoing a ritual process (a vow? disease quarantine?). Whatever its cause, his presence at the sanctuary during David’s visit is the fatal detail — he will report to Saul (1 Samuel 22:9-10) and massacre the priests. The narrator plants this narrative bomb quietly.
8: David asks for a weapon: ḥānîṯ ʾô-ḥereb — “a spear or sword.” kî gam-ḥarbî wəgam-kēlai lōʾ-lāqaḥtî bəyāḏî — “for I have brought neither my sword nor my equipment.” The haste of the flight is emphasized; the king’s champion is weaponless. The absence of David’s own sword sets up the provision of Goliath’s sword — a deeply ironic supply.
9: ḥereḇ Golyāṯ happəlištî ʾăšer hikkeṯā bəʿēmeq hāʾēlāh — “the sword of Goliath the Philistine whom you struck in the Valley of Elah — there it is, wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod.” Goliath’s sword is kept as a sacred trophy at the sanctuary. ʾaḥǎrê hāʾēpôḏ (behind the ephod) places it in the innermost zone of the tabernacle precinct. David receives back the sword of his greatest victory as a fugitive at his lowest point — the warrior is re-armed with his own trophy.
10: David flees to Achish king of Gath — the homeland of Goliath, carrying Goliath’s sword. The audacity of the move (seeking refuge among the Philistines) is remarkable; the risk is that he will be recognized. wayyāqom wayyibrāḥ bayyôm hahûʾ mippenê šāʾûl — the pace of flight is immediate; “that very day.”
11: Achish’s servants identify David: hălôʾ-zeh Dāwîḏ melek hāʾāreṣ — “is this not David the king of the land?” They quote the victory song: “Saul has struck his thousands, and David his ten thousands.” David’s fame has preceded him; the Philistines know his identity and his glory — making Gath the most dangerous possible refuge.
12: wayyāśem Dāwîḏ ʾeṯ-hadəḇārîm hāʾēlleh bilibbô — “David took these words to heart.” śûm bilbāb (to set in the heart) marks serious calculation. His fear of Achish is tactical — he recognized the danger and responded with the only tool available: performance.
13: wayyəšannēʾ ʾeṯ-ṭaʿmô bəʿênêhem wayyiṯhôlēl bəyādām — “he changed his behavior before them and acted as if insane.” šānāh (H8138, to change) with ṭaʿam (reason/taste/judgment) means deliberately altering his presentation. hiṯhôlēl (Hitpoel of hālal or hôlēl) = to feign madness. He makes marks on the doors and lets saliva run on his beard — both behaviors associated with mental breakdown in the ancient world.
14: Achish’s dismissal: rəʾû ʾîš mištaggēaʿ — “look, this man is insane.” šāgaʿ (H7696, to be mad) is the word used for ecstatic prophecy in negative contexts; the boundary between prophetic ecstasy and madness was culturally thin. Achish’s reaction — “why did you bring him to me?” — shows that a mad person was of no political value.
15: hăḥasēr məšuggāʿîm ʾānî — “Am I short of madmen?” Achish’s rhetorical question dismisses David as socially worthless. The irony is deep: the man who killed Achish’s greatest champion is dismissed as a worthless madman — survival through humiliation. The same pattern — apparent weakness concealing divine protection — runs throughout David’s wilderness period.
